subject: Mathematics
q:
The values of a and b, for which the system of equations

2x+3y+6z=8

x+2y+az=5

3x+5y+9z=b

has no solution, are :
question:
The values of a and b, for which the system of equations

2x+3y+6z=8

x+2y+az=5

3x+5y+9z=b

has no solution, are :
solution:
The given equation is 

2x+3y+6z=8

x+2y+az=5

3x+5y+9z=b

So, D=23612a359

=218-5a-39-3a+65-6

=36-10a-27+9a+30-36

=3-a

Dz=23812535 b

=22b-10-3b-15+85-6

=4b-20-3b+15+40-48

=b-13

The system of equation has no solution if D=0 & Dz≠0

So, If a=3, b≠13, then the system of equation has no solution.

q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mtable equalrows="true" equalcolumns="true"><mtr><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>2</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>3</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>4</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>3</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>4</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>5</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>5</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>8</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>10</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>17</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>A</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>B</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>C</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>D</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>C</mi></math>  is equal to :
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mtable equalrows="true" equalcolumns="true"><mtr><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>2</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>3</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>4</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>3</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>4</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>5</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>5</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>8</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>10</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>17</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>A</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>B</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>C</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>D</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>C</mi></math>  is equal to :
solution:
Δ=x−22x−33x−42x−33x−44x−53x−55x−810x−17

Applying R2→R2-R1, R3→R3-R2, we get

Δ=x−22x−33x−4x-1x−1x−1x−22x−26x−2=x-1x-2x−22x−33x−4111126

=-3x3+12x2-15x+6

∴B+C=12-15=-3

subject: Mathematics
q: The values of α, for which 132α+32113α+132α+33α+10=0, lie in the interval
question: The values of α, for which 132α+32113α+132α+33α+10=0, lie in the interval
solution:
Given: 132α+32113α+132α+33α+10=0

Applying, R1→R1-R2

⇒07676113α+132α+33α+10=0

Applying, C2→C2-C3

⇒00761-αα+132α+33α+10=0

⇒0-0+763α+1+2α2+3α=0

⇒2α2+6α+1=0

⇒α=-6±36-4212×2

⇒α=-6±274

⇒α=-3±72

⇒α≈-3±2.62

⇒α≈-0.42, -5.62

⇒α≈-0.2, -2.8

⇒α∈-3,0

subject: Mathematics
q:
If $\mathrm{f}(\theta)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & \cos \theta & 1 \\ -\sin \theta & 1 & -\cos \theta \\ -1 & \sin \theta & 1\end{array}\right|$ and
$\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are respectively the maximum and the minimum values of $f(\theta)$, then $(A, B)$ is equal to:
question:
If $\mathrm{f}(\theta)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & \cos \theta & 1 \\ -\sin \theta & 1 & -\cos \theta \\ -1 & \sin \theta & 1\end{array}\right|$ and
$\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are respectively the maximum and the minimum values of $f(\theta)$, then $(A, B)$ is equal to:
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text { Let } f(\theta)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & \cos \theta & 1 \\
-\sin \theta & 1 & -\cos \theta \\
-1 & \sin \theta & 1
\end{array}\right| \\
&=(1+\sin \theta \cos \theta)-\cos \theta(-\sin \theta-\cos \theta) \\
&+1\left(-\sin ^2 \theta+1\right) \\
&=1+\sin \theta \cos \theta+\sin \theta \cos \theta+ \\
&=2+2 \sin \theta \cos \theta+\cos 2 \theta \\
&=2+\sin 2 \theta+\cos 2 \theta
\end{aligned}
$$
Now, maximum value of (1)
is $2+\sqrt{1^2+1^2}=2+\sqrt{2}$ and minimum value of (1) is
$$
2-\sqrt{1^2+1^2}=2-\sqrt{2} \text {. }
$$

subject: Mathematics
q:
For the system of linear equations

2x-y+3z=5

3x+2y-z=7

4x+5y+αz=β,

which of the following is NOT correct?
question:
For the system of linear equations

2x-y+3z=5

3x+2y-z=7

4x+5y+αz=β,

which of the following is NOT correct?
options: The system has infinitely many solutions for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>–</mo><mn>5</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn></math>

The system has infinitely many solutions for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn></math>

The system in inconsistent for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>–</mo><mn>5</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn></math>

The system has a unique solution for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>≠</mo><mo>–</mo><mn>5</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn></math>
solution:
Given,

For the system of linear equations

2x-y+3z=5

3x+2y-z=7

4x+5y+αz=β,

Now finding,

D=2-1332-145α

⇒D=22α+5+3α+4+37

⇒D=7α+35

Now we know that, for unique solutionD≠0⇒α≠–5

So, in option B α=–6 corresponds to infinitely many solution, which is completely wrong hence, this is answer.

subject: Mathematics
q:
For the system of equations

x+y+z=6

x+2y+αz=10

x+3y+5z=β, which one of the following is NOT true?
question:
For the system of equations

x+y+z=6

x+2y+αz=10

x+3y+5z=β, which one of the following is NOT true?
options: System has a unique solution for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>14</mn></math>
System has infinitely many solutions for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>14</mn></math>
System has a unique solution for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>β</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>14</mn></math>
solution:
Given, the system of equations

x+y+z=6

x+2y+αz=10

x+3y+5z=β

Now finding,

△=11112α135

⇒△=110-3α-5-α+3-2

⇒△=6-2α

Now for unique solution, △≠0⇒α≠3

So, for unique solution ⇒α≠3

But in option D, system has unique solution for α=3 which is completely wrong.

q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>z</mi></math> are non-zero distinct real numbers, then<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>a</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>y</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>b</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>z</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>c</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>z</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math> is equal to :
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>z</mi></math> are non-zero distinct real numbers, then<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>a</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>y</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>b</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>z</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>c</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>z</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math> is equal to :
solution:
Given x+a=y+b=z+c+1

Now xa+ya+xyb+yb+yzc+yc+z=xa+yayb+ybzc+ycC3→C3-C1

=xyayybzycC2→C2-C3

=yx1ay1bz1c

R2→R2-R1 and R3→R3-R1

 yx1ay-x0b-az-x0c-a=yx1aa-b0-(a-b)z-x0c-a=y(a-b)x1a10-1z-x0c-a=-y(a-b)(c-a+z-x)=y(a-b)

subject: Mathematics
q: The least value of the product <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi><mi>z</mi></math> (such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi><mo> </mo><mtext>and</mtext><mo> </mo><mi>z</mi></math> are positive real numbers) for which the determinant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi>x</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable><mi> </mi><mi> </mi><mi> </mi><mi> </mi><mtable><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>y</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable><mi> </mi><mi> </mi><mtable><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>z</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></mrow></mfenced></math> is non-negative is
question: The least value of the product <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi><mi>z</mi></math> (such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi><mo> </mo><mtext>and</mtext><mo> </mo><mi>z</mi></math> are positive real numbers) for which the determinant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi>x</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable><mi> </mi><mi> </mi><mi> </mi><mi> </mi><mtable><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>y</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable><mi> </mi><mi> </mi><mtable><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>z</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></mrow></mfenced></math> is non-negative is
solution:
Since  A.M ≥G.M

x+y+z3 ≥xyz13

x+y+z ≥3xyz13

Now, x11 1y1 11z=xyz-x+y+z+2

Now determinant is non-negative

⇒xyz-x+y+z+2≥0

⇒xyz-3xyz13+2 ≥0

Let xyz=t3

So, t3-3t+2 ≥0

⇒t+2(t2-2t+1)≥0⇒t+2(t-1)2≥0

⇒t≥-2

⇒t3≥-8

⇒ Least value of xyz= -8

subject: Mathematics
q:
If $f: \mathbf{N} \rightarrow \mathbf{Z}$ is defined by
$f(n) = \begin{vmatrix} n & -1 & -5 \\ -2n^2 & 3(2k+1) & 2k+1 \\ -3n^3 & 3k(2k+1) & 3k(k+2)+1 \end{vmatrix}$, $k \in \mathbf{N}$,
and $\sum_{n=1}^{k} f(n) = 98$, then $k$ is equal to :
question:
If $f: \mathbf{N} \rightarrow \mathbf{Z}$ is defined by
$f(n) = \begin{vmatrix} n & -1 & -5 \\ -2n^2 & 3(2k+1) & 2k+1 \\ -3n^3 & 3k(2k+1) & 3k(k+2)+1 \end{vmatrix}$, $k \in \mathbf{N}$,
and $\sum_{n=1}^{k} f(n) = 98$, then $k$ is equal to :
solution:
Let $S = \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{k} f(n)$, where
$f(n) = \begin{vmatrix} n & -1 & -5 \\ -2n^2 & 3(2k+1) & 2k+1 \\ -3n^3 & 3k(2k+1) & 3k(k+2)+1 \end{vmatrix}$

Since only the first column depends on $n$, the summation can be taken inside that column:
$S = \begin{vmatrix} \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{k} n & -1 & -5 \\ \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{k} (-2n^2) & 3(2k+1) & 2k+1 \\ \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{k} (-3n^3) & 3k(2k+1) & 3k(k+2)+1 \end{vmatrix}$

Using standard summation formulas:
$\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{k} n = \dfrac{k(k+1)}{2}$
$\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{k} (-2n^2) = -\dfrac{k(k+1)(2k+1)}{3}$
$\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{k} (-3n^3) = -\dfrac{3k^2(k+1)^2}{4}$

Substituting:
$S = \begin{vmatrix} \dfrac{k(k+1)}{2} & -1 & -5 \\ -\dfrac{k(k+1)(2k+1)}{3} & 3(2k+1) & 2k+1 \\ -\dfrac{3k^2(k+1)^2}{4} & 3k(2k+1) & 3k^2+6k+1 \end{vmatrix}$

Taking $\dfrac{k(k+1)}{12}$ common from $C_1$:
$S = \dfrac{k(k+1)}{12} \begin{vmatrix} 6 & -1 & -5 \\ -4(2k+1) & 3(2k+1) & 2k+1 \\ -9k(k+1) & 3k(2k+1) & 3k^2+6k+1 \end{vmatrix}$

Applying $C_1 \to C_1 + C_2 + C_3$:
Row 1: $6 - 1 - 5 = 0$
Row 2: $-8k - 4 + 6k + 3 + 2k + 1 = 0$
Row 3: $-9k^2 - 9k + 6k^2 + 3k + 3k^2 + 6k + 1 = 1$

$S = \dfrac{k(k+1)}{12} \begin{vmatrix} 0 & -1 & -5 \\ 0 & 3(2k+1) & 2k+1 \\ 1 & 3k(2k+1) & 3k^2+6k+1 \end{vmatrix}$

Expanding along $C_1$:
$S = \dfrac{k(k+1)}{12} \cdot 1 \cdot \begin{vmatrix} -1 & -5 \\ 3(2k+1) & 2k+1 \end{vmatrix}$

$S = \dfrac{k(k+1)}{12}\left[(-1)(2k+1) - (-5)(3(2k+1))\right]$

$S = \dfrac{k(k+1)}{12}\left[-(2k+1) + 15(2k+1)\right]$

$S = \dfrac{k(k+1)}{12} \cdot 14(2k+1) = 7 \cdot \dfrac{k(k+1)(2k+1)}{6}$

Note that $\dfrac{k(k+1)(2k+1)}{6} = \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{k} n^2$.

Given $S = 98$:
$7 \cdot \dfrac{k(k+1)(2k+1)}{6} = 98$

$\dfrac{k(k+1)(2k+1)}{6} = 14$

Checking integer values of $k$:
$k = 1$: sum $= 1$
$k = 2$: sum $= 5$
$k = 3$: sum $= 14$

Hence, $k = 3$, and the correct option is $(1)\ 3$.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $\alpha, \beta(\alpha \neq \beta)$ be the values of m , for which the equations $x+y+z=1 ; x+2 y+4 z=\mathrm{m}$ and $x+4 y+10 z=m^2$ have infinitely many solutions. Then the value of $\sum_{n=1}^{10}\left(n^\alpha+n^\beta\right)$ is equal to :
question: Let $\alpha, \beta(\alpha \neq \beta)$ be the values of m , for which the equations $x+y+z=1 ; x+2 y+4 z=\mathrm{m}$ and $x+4 y+10 z=m^2$ have infinitely many solutions. Then the value of $\sum_{n=1}^{10}\left(n^\alpha+n^\beta\right)$ is equal to :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
\Delta & =\left|\begin{array}{llc}
1 & 1 & 1 \\
1 & 2 & 4 \\
1 & 4 & 10
\end{array}\right|=1(20-16)-1(10-4)+1(4-2) \\
& =4-6+2=0
\end{aligned}$

For infinite solutions
$\begin{aligned}
& \Delta_x=\Delta_y=\Delta_z=0 \\
& m^2-3 x+2=0 \\
& m=1,2 \\
& \alpha=1, \beta=2 \\
& \therefore \sum_{n=1}^{10}\left(n^\alpha+n^\beta\right)=\sum_{n=1}^{10} n^1+\sum_{n=1}^{10} n^2 \\
&= \frac{10(11)}{2}+\frac{10(11)(21)}{6} \\
&= 55+385 \\
&= 440
\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q:
The values of $m, n$, for which the system of equations
$\begin{aligned}
& x+y+z=4, \\
& 2 x+5 y+5 z=17, \\
& x+2 y+\mathrm{m} z=\mathrm{n}
\end{aligned}$
has infinitely many solutions, satisfy the equation:
question:
The values of $m, n$, for which the system of equations
$\begin{aligned}
& x+y+z=4, \\
& 2 x+5 y+5 z=17, \\
& x+2 y+\mathrm{m} z=\mathrm{n}
\end{aligned}$
has infinitely many solutions, satisfy the equation:
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{D}=\left|\begin{array}{lll}1 & 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & 5 \\ 1 & 2 & \mathrm{~m}\end{array}\right|=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{m}=2 \\ & \mathrm{D}_3=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & 4 \\ 2 & 5 & 17 \\ 1 & 2 & \mathrm{n}\end{array}\right|=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=7\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let the system of linear equations

x+y+kz=2

2x+3y-z=1

3x+4y+2z=k

have infinitely many solutions. Then the system

k+1 x+2k-1 y=7

2k+1x+k+5y=10 has :
question:
Let the system of linear equations

x+y+kz=2

2x+3y-z=1

3x+4y+2z=k

have infinitely many solutions. Then the system

k+1 x+2k-1 y=7

2k+1x+k+5y=10 has :
solution:
Linear equations x+y+kz=2, 2x+3y-z=1 and 3x+4y+2z=k have infinitely many solutions.

Therefore,

11k23-1342=0

⇒110-17+k-1=0

⇒10-7-k=0

⇒3-k=0

⇒k=3

For k=3, the second system of equations is

4x+5y=7        …1

7x+8y=10      …2

Clearly, they have a unique solution

Subtracting 1 & 2, we get

3x+3y=3

⇒x+y=1

subject: Mathematics
q:
If the system of equations

kx+y+2z=1

3x-y-2z=2

-2x-2y-4z=3

has infinitely many solutions, then k is equal to ______ .
question:
If the system of equations

kx+y+2z=1

3x-y-2z=2

-2x-2y-4z=3

has infinitely many solutions, then k is equal to ______ .
solution:
If the system of equations

P1: kx+y+2z=1

P2: 3x-y-2z=2

P3: -2x-2y-4z=3

Which are equivalent to equations of planes.

We observe 5P2-P1=3P3

So, 15-K=-6

⇒ K=21

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let $\alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{R}$ be such that the system of linear equations
$x + 2y + z = 5$
$2x + y + \alpha z = 5$
$8x + 4y + \beta z = 18$
has no solution. Then $\dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}$ is equal to :
question:
Let $\alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{R}$ be such that the system of linear equations
$x + 2y + z = 5$
$2x + y + \alpha z = 5$
$8x + 4y + \beta z = 18$
has no solution. Then $\dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}$ is equal to :
solution:
The given system of linear equations is:
$x + 2y + z = 5$
$2x + y + \alpha z = 5$
$8x + 4y + \beta z = 18$

For the system to have no solution, the determinant of the coefficient matrix $D$ must be zero.

$D = \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & \alpha \\ 8 & 4 & \beta \end{vmatrix} = 0$

Expanding along the first row:

$1(\beta - 4\alpha) - 2(2\beta - 8\alpha) + 1(8 - 8) = 0$

$\beta - 4\alpha - 4\beta + 16\alpha = 0$

$12\alpha - 3\beta = 0$

$\beta = 4\alpha$

To verify the no solution condition, substitute $\beta = 4\alpha$ into the third equation:

$8x + 4y + 4\alpha z = 18$

$4(2x + y + \alpha z) = 18$

$2x + y + \alpha z = \dfrac{9}{2}$

However, the second equation is $2x + y + \alpha z = 5$. This results in a contradiction since $\dfrac{9}{2} \neq 5$. Thus, the system has no solution when $\beta = 4\alpha$.

Therefore, $\dfrac{\beta}{\alpha} = 4$.

Answer: $4$

x+αy+3z=-4

3x-y+βz=7 have many solutions,

So, △=△1=△2=△3

Now, finding △2=25-11-4337β=0

⇒2-4β-21-5β-9-17+12=0

⇒13β=-16

Now, finding △3=2351α-43-17=0

⇒14α-8-57-5-15α=0

⇒α=-70

Hence, the value of 13αβ=70×16=1120

solution:

1 + 1 + 1 1 + α + β 1 + α 2 + β 2 1 + α + β 1 + α 2 + β 2 1 + α 3 + β 3 1 + α 2 + β 2 1 + α 3 + β 3 1 + α 4 + β 4 =1111αβ1α2β21111αα21ββ2

= α β β - α - β - α β + α + β - α 2

=β-a2αβ-β-α+12

  = β - α 2 α - 1 2 β - 1 2

Hence, K=1.

subject: Mathematics
q:
If the system of equations
$\begin{array}{r}
11 x+y+\lambda z=-5 \\
2 x+3 y+5 z=3 \\
8 x-19 y-39 z=\mu
\end{array}$
has infinitely many solutions, then $\lambda^4-\mu$ is equal to :
question:
If the system of equations
$\begin{array}{r}
11 x+y+\lambda z=-5 \\
2 x+3 y+5 z=3 \\
8 x-19 y-39 z=\mu
\end{array}$
has infinitely many solutions, then $\lambda^4-\mu$ is equal to :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& 11 x+y+\lambda z=-5 \\
& 2 x+3 y+5 z=3 \\
& 8 x-19 y-39 z=\mu
\end{aligned}$
for infinite sol.
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{D}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
11 & 1 & \lambda \\
2 & 3 & 5 \\
8 & -19 & -39
\end{array}\right|=0 \\
& \Rightarrow 11(-117+95)-1(-78-40)+\lambda(-38-24) \\
& \Rightarrow 11(-22)+118-\lambda(62)=0 \\
& \Rightarrow 62 \lambda=118-242 \\
& \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{-124}{62}=-2
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{D}_1=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}-5 & 1 & -2 \\ 3 & 3 & 5 \\ \mu & -19 & -39\end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-5(-117+95)-1(-117-5 \mu)-2(-57-3 \mu)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-5(-22)+117+5 \mu+114+6 \mu=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 11 \mu=-110-231=-341 \\ & \Rightarrow \mu=-31 \\ & \lambda^4-\mu=(-2)^4-(-31)=16+31=47\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q:
Consider the system of linear equations in $x, y, z$:
$x + 2y + tz = 0$,
$6x + y + 5tz = 0$,
$3x + t^2 y + f(t) z = 0$,
where $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is a differentiable function. If this system has infinitely many solutions for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$, then $f$
question:
Consider the system of linear equations in $x, y, z$:
$x + 2y + tz = 0$,
$6x + y + 5tz = 0$,
$3x + t^2 y + f(t) z = 0$,
where $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is a differentiable function. If this system has infinitely many solutions for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$, then $f$
solution:
For a homogeneous system of linear equations to have infinitely many solutions, the determinant of its coefficient matrix must be zero.

The coefficient matrix is:
$\Delta = \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 2 & t \\ 6 & 1 & 5t \\ 3 & t^2 & f(t) \end{vmatrix}$

Expanding the determinant along the first row, we get:
$\Delta = 1(1 \cdot f(t) - 5t \cdot t^2) - 2(6 \cdot f(t) - 3 \cdot 5t) + t(6 \cdot t^2 - 3 \cdot 1)$

$\Delta = f(t) - 5t^3 - 12f(t) + 30t + 6t^3 - 3t$

$\Delta = -11f(t) + t^3 + 27t$

Since the system has infinitely many solutions for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$, we must have $\Delta = 0$ for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$.

$-11f(t) + t^3 + 27t = 0$

$f(t) = \dfrac{t^3 + 27t}{11}$

To determine the nature of the function $f(t)$, we find its derivative with respect to $t$:

$f'(t) = \dfrac{3t^2 + 27}{11}$

Since $t^2 \ge 0$ for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$, we have $3t^2 + 27 \ge 27 > 0$.

Therefore, $f'(t) > 0$ for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$, which implies that $f(t)$ is a strictly increasing function on $\mathbb{R}$.

Answer: is strictly increasing on $\mathbb{R}$

Then the number of elements in S, is:

C3→C3-C2,  C2→C2-C1 ( Let α is common ratio of GP)

logea1ra2klogeαr+klogeαr+klogea4ra5klogeαr+kloge⁡αr+klogea7ra8kloge⁡αr+kloge⁡αr+k=0 which is always true because C2 & C3 are identical.

q: Let for any three distinct consecutive terms a, b, c of an A.P, the lines ax+by+c=0 be concurrent at the point P and Q(α,β) be a point such that the system of equations x+y+z=6, 2x+5y+αz=β and x+2 y+3 z=4, has infinitely many solutions. Then (PQ)2 is equal to _______.
question: Let for any three distinct consecutive terms a, b, c of an A.P, the lines ax+by+c=0 be concurrent at the point P and Q(α,β) be a point such that the system of equations x+y+z=6, 2x+5y+αz=β and x+2 y+3 z=4, has infinitely many solutions. Then (PQ)2 is equal to _______.
solution:
Given,

a,b,c are in A.P.

⇒2b=a+c⇒a-2b+c=0

Now, on comparing above equation with ax+by+c=0 we get,

 The line ax+by+c=0 passes through fixed point 1,-2

∴P=(1,-2)

Now, we know that for infinite solution,

D=D1=D2=D3=0

D=11125α123=0

⇒α=8

And D1=611β5α423=0⇒β=6

So, the point Q=α,β=(8,6)

Hence, PQ2=72+82=49+64=113

has :
question:

x+cosγy+cosβz=0

cosγx+y+cosαz=0

cosβx+cosαy+z=0

has :
solution:
Given, α+β+γ=2π

Now, Δ=1cosγcosβcosγ1cosαcosβcosα1

By expanding along the first column, we get

∆=11-cos2α-cosγcosγ-cosα.cosβ+cosβcosα.cosγ-cosβ

∆=1-cos2α-cos2γ+cosγ.cosα.cosβ+cosβ.cosα.cosγ-cos2β

=1-cos2α-cos2β-cos2γ+2cosα.cosβ.cosγ

=sin2α-cos2β-cosγ(cosγ-2cosα.cosβ)

=-cos(α+β).cos(α-β)-cosγcos2π-(α+β)-2cosα.cosβ

=-cos(2π-γ)cos(α-β)-cosγcos(α+β)-2cosα.cosβ

=-cos(2π-γ).cos(α-β)-cosγcosα.cosβ-sinα.sinβ-2cosα.cosβ

=-cosγ.cos(α-β)+cosγ.cos(α-β)

=0

So, ∆=0

Hence, the system of equations has infinitely many solutions.

q:
The set of all values of λ for which the system of linear equations:
2 x 1 - 2 x 2 + x 3 = λ x 1
2 x 1 - 3 x 2 + 2 x 3 = λ x 2
- x 1 + 2 x 2 = λ x 3
has a non-trivial solution,
question:
The set of all values of λ for which the system of linear equations:
2 x 1 - 2 x 2 + x 3 = λ x 1
2 x 1 - 3 x 2 + 2 x 3 = λ x 2
- x 1 + 2 x 2 = λ x 3
has a non-trivial solution,
solution:
2 - λ x 1 - 2 x 2 + x 3 = 0

2 x 1 - λ + 3 x 2 + 2 x 3 = 0

- x 1 + 2 x 2 - λ x 3 = 0

The systems of linear equations will have a non-trivial solution

⇒     2 - λ - 2 1 2 - λ - 3 2 - 1 2 - λ = 0

⇒     2 - λ λ 2 + 3 λ - 4 + 2 - 2 λ + 2 + 1 4 - λ - 3 = 0

2 λ 2 + 6 λ - 8 - λ 3 - 3 λ 2 + 4 λ - 4 λ + 4 + 1 - λ = 0

- λ 3 - λ 2 + 5 λ - 3 = 0

λ 3 + λ 2 - 5 λ + 3 = 0

λ - 1 λ 2 + 2 λ - 3 = 0

λ - 1 λ + 3 λ - 1 = 0

⇒    λ = -3 , 1 , 1

q:
If the system of equations
$\begin{aligned}
& 2 x-y+z=4 \\
& 5 x+\lambda y+3 z=12 \\
& 100 x-47 y+\mu z=212
\end{aligned}$
has infinitely many solutions, then $\mu-2 \lambda$ is equal to
question:
If the system of equations
$\begin{aligned}
& 2 x-y+z=4 \\
& 5 x+\lambda y+3 z=12 \\
& 100 x-47 y+\mu z=212
\end{aligned}$
has infinitely many solutions, then $\mu-2 \lambda$ is equal to
solution: $\begin{aligned} & 2 x-y+z=4 \\ & 5 x+\lambda y+3 z=12 \\ & 100 x-47 y+\mu z=212 \\ & \Delta x=\Delta y=\Delta z=0 \\ & \Delta z=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}2 & -1 & 4 \\ 5 & \lambda & 12 \\ 100 & -47 & 212\end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2(212 \lambda+564)+1(1060-1200) \\ & \Rightarrow 424 \lambda+1128-140-940-400 \lambda=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=-2 \\ & \quad \Delta y=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}2 & -1 & 1 \\ 5 & -2 & 3 \\ 100 & -47 & \mu\end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2(-2 \mu+141)+1(5 \mu-300)+1(-235+200)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \mu=53 \\ & \mu-2 \lambda=53-2(-2)=57\end{aligned}$

q:
If the system of equations
$\begin{aligned} & (\lambda-1) x+(\lambda-4) y+\lambda z=5 \\ & \lambda x+(\lambda-1) y+(\lambda-4) z=7 \\ & (\lambda+1) x+(\lambda+2) y-(\lambda+2) z=9\end{aligned}$
has infinitely many solutions, then $\lambda^2+\lambda$ is equal to
question:
If the system of equations
$\begin{aligned} & (\lambda-1) x+(\lambda-4) y+\lambda z=5 \\ & \lambda x+(\lambda-1) y+(\lambda-4) z=7 \\ & (\lambda+1) x+(\lambda+2) y-(\lambda+2) z=9\end{aligned}$
has infinitely many solutions, then $\lambda^2+\lambda$ is equal to
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& (\lambda-1) x+(\lambda-4) y+\lambda z=5 \\
& \lambda x+(\lambda-1) y+(\lambda-4) z=7 \\
& (\lambda+1) x+(\lambda+2) y-(\lambda+2) z=9
\end{aligned}$

For infinitely many solutions
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{D}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
\lambda-1 & \lambda-4 & \lambda \\
\lambda & \lambda-1 & \lambda-4 \\
\lambda+1 & \lambda+2 & -(\lambda+2)
\end{array}\right|=0 \\
& (\lambda-3)(2 \lambda+1)=0 \\
& \mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{x}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
5 & \lambda-4 & \lambda \\
7 & \lambda-1 & \lambda-4 \\
9 & \lambda+2 & -(\lambda+2)
\end{array}\right|=0 \\
& 2(3-\lambda)(23-2 \lambda)=0 \\
& \lambda=3 \\
& \therefore \lambda^2+\lambda=9+3=12
\end{aligned}$

question: Let α be a root of the equation a-cx2+b-ax+c-b=0 where a, b, c are distinct real numbers such that the matrix α2α1111abc is singular. Then the value of  a-c2b-ac-b+b-a2a-cc-b+c-b2a-cb-a is

question: If fx=x32x2+11+3x3x2+22xx3+6x3−x4x2−2 for all x∈ℝ, then 2f0+f'0 is equal to
solution:
Given: fx=x32x2+11+3x3x2+22xx3+6x3-x4x2-2

⇒f0=01120604-2

⇒f0=0+4+8

⇒f0=12

⇒f'x=x32x2+11+3x3x2+22xx3+63x2-102x+x32x2+11+3x6x23x2x3-x4x2-2+3x24x33x2+22xx3+6x3-x4x2-2

q:
If a, b, c  are non - zero real numbers and if the system of equations

a-1x=y+z

b-1y=x+z

c-1z=x+y

has a non - trivial solution, then ab+bc+ca  equals :
question:
If a, b, c  are non - zero real numbers and if the system of equations

a-1x=y+z

b-1y=x+z

c-1z=x+y

has a non - trivial solution, then ab+bc+ca  equals :
solution:
Given, a-1x=y+z⇒a-1x-y-z=0

b-1y=z+x⇒-x+b-1y-z=0

c-1z=y+x=-x-y+c-1z=0

For a non-trivial solution

a-1-1-1-1b-1-1-1-1c-1=0

⇒   1-a1111-b1111-c=0

⇒   1-a1-b1-c-1-11-c-1+1-1-b=0

⇒   1-a+b+c+ab+bc+ca-abc-1+a+b+c=0

⇒ab+bc+ca=abc

subject: Mathematics
q:
The system of linear equations
$x+y+z=6$
$2 x+5 y+a z=36$
$x+2 y+3 z=b$
has
question:
The system of linear equations
$x+y+z=6$
$2 x+5 y+a z=36$
$x+2 y+3 z=b$
has
solution:
Coefficient matrix determinant: $\Delta = 1(15-2a) - 1(6-a) + 1(4-5) = 8 - a$.
For $a = 8$, $\Delta = 0$, so unique solution is not possible.
With $a = 8$: $R_2 - 2R_1$ gives $3y + 6z = 24 \Rightarrow y + 2z = 8$.
$R_3 - R_1$ gives $y + 2z = b - 6$.
For consistency: $b - 6 = 8 \Rightarrow b = 14$.
When $a = 8, b = 14$: the system has infinitely many solutions.
